examples.explanation:
Start at index 0 (value 2).
From index 0, can jump to indices 1 or 2.
Option 1: Jump to index 1 (value 3).
  From index 1, can jump to indices 2, 3, or 4.
  Option 1a: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
    From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
      From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 4 (0→1→2→3→4).
  Option 1b: Jump to index 3 (value 1).
    From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 3 (0→1→3→4).
  Option 1c: Jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 2 (0→1→4).
  Minimum from index 1: min(4,3,2) = 2 jumps.
Option 2: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
  From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
    From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 3 (0→2→3→4).
Minimum from index 0: min(2+1, 3+1) = min(3,4) = 2 jumps.

Start at index 0 (value 3).
From index 0, can jump to indices 1, 2, or 3.
Option 1: Jump to index 1 (value 2).
  From index 1, can jump to indices 2 or 3.
  Option 1a: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
    From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
      From index 3 (value 0), cannot jump anywhere. Dead end.
  Option 1b: Jump to index 3 (value 0). Dead end.
Option 2: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
  From index 2, can jump to index 3 only. Dead end.
Option 3: Jump to index 3 (value 0). Dead end.
All paths lead to dead ends at index 3, so no path to end. Return infinity or -1.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible jump sequences from each index, tracking the minimum jumps to reach the end. Use it for understanding the problem but not for efficient solutions due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Minimum Jumps
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all jump sequences to find minimum jumps to reach end.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution for Jump Game II. The intuition is to treat the problem as exploring a decision tree where at each position i, we can jump to any position from i+1 to i+nums[i]. We recursively explore all possible paths from the current position to the end, keeping track of the minimum jumps needed. The base case is when we reach or exceed the last index (n-1), where we return 0 jumps. For each position, we iterate through all possible jump lengths, make recursive calls, and take the minimum of all results plus 1 (for the current jump). The time complexity is O(2^n) because in worst case, each position can jump to many subsequent positions, creating exponential branching. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach helps understand the problem structure but is impractical for large inputs due to exponential time. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive helper function from L1 as its core component. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no path exists (should return infinity), off-by-one errors in jump ranges, and not adding 1 for the current jump when taking minimum. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that takes current index, returns min jumps from there to end, with memoization optional but not changing worst-case complexity.

examples.trace:
Start at index 0 (value 2).
From index 0, can jump to indices 1 or 2.
Option 1: Jump to index 1 (value 3).
  From index 1, can jump to indices 2, 3, or 4.
  Option 1a: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
    From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
      From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 4 (0→1→2→3→4).
  Option 1b: Jump to index 3 (value 1).
    From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 3 (0→1→3→4).
  Option 1c: Jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 2 (0→1→4).
  Minimum from index 1: min(4,3,2) = 2 jumps.
Option 2: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
  From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
    From index 3, can jump to index 4 (end). Total jumps: 3 (0→2→3→4).
Minimum from index 0: min(2+1, 3+1) = min(3,4) = 2 jumps.

Start at index 0 (value 3).
From index 0, can jump to indices 1, 2, or 3.
Option 1: Jump to index 1 (value 2).
  From index 1, can jump to indices 2 or 3.
  Option 1a: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
    From index 2, can jump to index 3 only.
      From index 3 (value 0), cannot jump anywhere. Dead end.
  Option 1b: Jump to index 3 (value 0). Dead end.
Option 2: Jump to index 2 (value 1).
  From index 2, can jump to index 3 only. Dead end.
Option 3: Jump to index 3 (value 0). Dead end.
All paths lead to dead ends at index 3, so no path to end. Return infinity or -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Jump Helper Function
ladders.desc: Recursive function that returns minimum jumps from current index to end.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function used in the brute force approach. The intuition is to break down the problem into subproblems: from any index i, the minimum jumps to reach the end equals 1 plus the minimum of minimum jumps from all reachable next indices. The function takes current index i and array nums, returns min jumps from i to last index. Base case: if i >= n-1 (reached or passed end), return 0. Recursive case: initialize minJumps to infinity. For each possible next index j from i+1 to i+nums[i] (within bounds), make recursive call to get min jumps from j, take minimum of all results, add 1 for current jump, return that value. If no valid jumps (nums[i]==0 and i<n-1), return infinity. Time complexity for this function at a given i is O(nums[i]) times recursive complexity, but overall leads to O(2^n). Space: O(n) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main solution starting at index 0. Connection to L2: This implements the recursive decision-making logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add 1 for current jump, incorrect base case (should be i>=n-1 not i==n-1), not handling infinity properly. Real code pattern: def minJumpsFrom(i, nums): if i>=len(nums)-1: return 0; min_jumps=float('inf'); for j in range(i+1, min(i+nums[i]+1, len(nums))): min_jumps=min(min_jumps, 1+minJumpsFrom(j, nums)); return min_jumps.

examples.trace:
Call minJumpsFrom(0, [2,3,1,1,4]).
i=0 < n-1=4, nums[0]=2.
Initialize min_jumps=inf.
Possible j: 1 and 2.
For j=1: recursive call minJumpsFrom(1,...).
  i=1, nums[1]=3.
  Initialize min_jumps1=inf.
  Possible j: 2,3,4.
  For j=2: recursive call minJumpsFrom(2,...).
    i=2, nums[2]=1.
    Initialize min_jumps2=inf.
    Possible j: 3 only.
    For j=3: recursive call minJumpsFrom(3,...).
      i=3, nums[3]=1.
      Initialize min_jumps3=inf.
      Possible j: 4 only.
      For j=4: recursive call minJumpsFrom(4,...).
        i=4 >=4, return 0.
      min_jumps3 = min(inf, 1+0)=1.
    min_jumps2 = min(inf, 1+1)=2.
  For j=3: recursive call minJumpsFrom(3,...) returns 1 as above.
    min_jumps1 = min(2, 1+1)=2.
  For j=4: recursive call minJumpsFrom(4,...) returns 0.
    min_jumps1 = min(2, 1+0)=1.
  Return 1 from i=1.
Back to i=0: min_jumps = min(inf, 1+1)=2.
For j=2: recursive call minJumpsFrom(2,...) returns 2 as above.
  min_jumps = min(2, 1+2)=2.
Return 2.

Call minJumpsFrom(2, [1,0,2,0,1]).
i=2 < n-1=4, nums[2]=2.
Initialize min_jumps=inf.
Possible j: 3 and 4.
For j=3: recursive call minJumpsFrom(3,...).
  i=3, nums[3]=0.
  i=3 <4, but nums[3]=0, no possible jumps.
  Return inf.
For j=4: recursive call minJumpsFrom(4,...).
  i=4 >=4, return 0.
min_jumps = min(inf, 1+inf, 1+0) = min(inf, inf, 1) = 1.
But wait: from i=2, jump to j=4 is valid? i+nums[i]=2+2=4, so j=4 is reachable. Actually j range is i+1 to i+nums[i] inclusive, so j=4 is valid. So output should be 1, not inf. Let me correct: For j=4, recursive call returns 0, so min_jumps becomes 1. Return 1. My earlier trace was incorrect; corrected: min_jumps=min(inf, 1+inf, 1+0)=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decision Tree Exploration
ladders.desc: The recursive branching logic to explore all jump possibilities.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive decision-making process. The intuition is that at each position, we face multiple choices (jump lengths), and we need to explore all possible sequences to find the optimal one. This creates a decision tree where each node represents a position, and edges represent jumps. The algorithm: From current index i with jump capacity nums[i], we can move to any index j where i < j <= i+nums[i] and j < n. For each such j, we recursively solve the subproblem from j. The minimum jumps from i is 1 + minimum of results from all j. This is a classic recursive backtracking pattern. Time complexity arises from the branching factor: at each step, up to nums[i] branches, leading to exponential possibilities in worst case. Space complexity is the recursion depth, up to n. Connection to L1: This logic is encapsulated in the helper function's loop and recursive calls. Connection to L3: This uses the concept of iterating through possible next indices. Common mistakes: Not limiting j to array bounds, incorrect handling of when nums[i]==0, off-by-one in range (should be i+1 to i+nums[i] inclusive). Real code pattern: for j in range(i+1, min(i+nums[i]+1, len(nums))): explore j recursively.

examples.trace:
At i=0, nums[0]=2. Possible j: 1 and 2.
Branch 1: j=1.
  At i=1, nums[1]=1. Possible j: 2 only.
    At i=2, nums[2]=1. Possible j: 3 only.
      At i=3, reached end. Backtrack: jumps=3 (0→1→2→3).
Branch 2: j=2.
  At i=2, nums[2]=1. Possible j: 3 only.
    At i=3, reached end. Backtrack: jumps=2 (0→2→3).
Take minimum: min(3,2)=2 jumps.

At i=0, nums[0]=1. Possible j: 1 only.
  At i=1, nums[1]=2. Possible j: 2 and 3.
    Branch 1: j=2.
      At i=2, nums[2]=3. Possible j: 3,4,5 (5 out of bounds).
        Branch 1a: j=3.
          At i=3, nums[3]=4. Possible j: 4,5,6,7 (only 4 valid).
            At i=4, reached end. Jumps: 4 (0→1→2→3→4).
        Branch 1b: j=4.
          At i=4, reached end. Jumps: 3 (0→1→2→4).
    Branch 2: j=3.
      At i=3, nums[3]=4. Possible j: 4 only.
        At i=4, reached end. Jumps: 3 (0→1→3→4).
Minimum: min(4,3,3)=3 jumps.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Possible Next Indices
ladders.desc: Determining and iterating through all indices reachable from current position.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves calculating which indices can be reached from the current index i given nums[i]. The intuition is that from index i, you can jump to any index j such that i < j ≤ i + nums[i], provided j is within array bounds [0, n-1]. This defines the reachable set. The algorithm: Given i and nums, compute start = i+1, end = min(i+nums[i], n-1). Then iterate j from start to end inclusive. This is a fundamental step in the recursive exploration. Time complexity for this operation alone is O(nums[i]) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration is used in the recursive decision tree to generate branches. Connection to L4: This uses basic loop operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check array bounds (j < n), incorrect range (should be i+1 not i), off-by-one with inclusive/exclusive ranges. Real code pattern: for j in range(i+1, min(i+nums[i]+1, len(nums))): process j.

examples.trace:
i=0, nums[0]=2.
Compute start = i+1 = 1.
Compute end = min(i+nums[i], n-1) = min(0+2, 4) = min(2,4)=2.
Since range in Python is exclusive, use range(start, end+1) = range(1,3).
So j takes values 1 and 2.

i=3, nums[3]=4.
start = i+1 = 4.
end = min(i+nums[i], n-1) = min(3+4, 4) = min(7,4)=4.
range(4, 5) gives j=4 only.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and ensuring indices are within valid range.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves safely accessing elements in an array using indices. The intuition is that arrays have fixed size n with indices from 0 to n-1. Accessing outside this range causes errors. In the jump problem, we need to access nums[i] to know jump capacity, and we need to ensure any index j we consider is within 0 ≤ j < n. The algorithm: Before accessing nums[i], check if 0 ≤ i < len(nums). Similarly, when computing j from i+1 to i+nums[i], ensure j < len(nums). This prevents index out-of-bounds errors. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is used when iterating through possible next indices to avoid invalid accesses. Connection to L5: This builds on basic array concepts. Common mistakes: Assuming array indices start at 1, forgetting to check upper bound, off-by-one errors in loop conditions. Real code pattern: if i < 0 or i >= len(nums): handle error; similarly for j.

examples.trace:
Array has indices 0,1,2,3.
Check: 2 >=0 and 2 <4 (len=4), so valid.
Access nums[2] = 30.

examples.input: Check if j=5 is valid for array of length 5
examples.trace:
Array length n=5, valid indices are 0,1,2,3,4.
j=5 is not <5, so invalid.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners. An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type. Elements are ordered and each has an index, starting from 0. For example, an array nums of length n has elements nums[0], nums[1], ..., nums[n-1]. The intuition is that arrays allow random access: you can directly access any element if you know its index, in O(1) time. In the jump problem, the array nums represents jump capacities at each position. Key operations: declaring an array, accessing elements via index, knowing array length. Time for access: O(1). Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: This is the basis for safe array indexing. Common mistakes: Confusing index with value (e.g., thinking nums[2] means the value 2), thinking indices start at 1, not understanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, list is used as array: nums = [2,3,1]; x = nums[0] gives 2.

examples.trace:
Array has length 3.
Index 0: value 5.
Index 1: value 10.
Index 2: value 15.
Access nums[1] returns 10.

Length=1.
Only index 0 is valid, value 100.
nums[0] = 100.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use memoization to store the minimum jumps from each index to the end, avoiding recomputation in recursion. Apply it when a top-down DP approach is preferred for clarity over greedy methods.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for memo array and recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Jumps to End with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with memoization to find minimum jumps from start to end.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index of an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The DP with memoization approach solves this by recursively exploring all possible jumps from each index while storing computed results to avoid redundant calculations. The intuition is that the minimum jumps from index i to the end can be expressed as 1 plus the minimum of jumps from all reachable positions from i. By memoizing results, we transform an exponential brute-force recursion into O(n²) time complexity. The algorithm starts from index 0 and recursively computes jumps for each position, using a memo array to store results. If we reach the last index, we return 0 jumps. For each index, we check all possible jumps (1 to nums[i]) and take the minimum. The base case is when we're at or beyond the last index. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because each index may check up to n other indices. Space complexity is O(n) for memo array and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the recursive helper function with memoization as its core component. Common mistakes include not handling unreachable cases properly (returning a large number instead of infinity), forgetting to add 1 for the current jump, or incorrect bounds checking. Real code pattern: def minJumps(nums, i, memo): if i >= len(nums)-1: return 0; if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; min_jumps = float('inf'); for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if i+j < len(nums): jumps = 1 + minJumps(nums, i+j, memo); min_jumps = min(min_jumps, jumps); memo[i] = min_jumps; return memo[i];

examples.trace: Start at index 0 (value 2). From index 0, can jump 1 or 2 steps. Option 1: Jump 1 to index 1. From index 1 (value 3), can jump 1, 2, or 3 steps. Jump 1 to index 2 (value 1), then jump 1 to index 3 (value 1), then jump 1 to index 4 (end). That's 3 jumps. Jump 2 from index 1 to index 3, then jump 1 to index 4: 2 jumps. Jump 3 from index 1 to index 4: 1 jump from index 1. Total from index 0 via index 1: 1 (jump to index 1) + 1 (jump to end) = 2 jumps. Option 2: Jump 2 from index 0 to index 2. From index 2 (value 1), jump 1 to index 3, then jump 1 to index 4: 2 jumps from index 2. Total: 1 (jump to index 2) + 2 = 3 jumps. Minimum is 2. Memo array: memo[4]=0, memo[3]=1, memo[2]=2, memo[1]=1, memo[0]=2.

Start at index 0 (value 3). From index 0, can jump 1, 2, or 3 steps. Option 1: Jump 1 to index 1 (value 2). From index 1, can jump 1 or 2 steps. Jump 1 to index 2 (value 1). From index 2, jump 1 to index 3 (value 0). From index 3, cannot jump (value 0). Dead end. Jump 2 from index 1 to index 3: same dead end. Option 2: Jump 2 from index 0 to index 2. From index 2, jump 1 to index 3: dead end. Option 3: Jump 3 from index 0 to index 3: dead end. All paths lead to index 3 which has value 0, so unreachable. Return infinity, which translates to -1. Memo array: memo[4]=0, memo[3]=inf, memo[2]=inf, memo[1]=inf, memo[0]=inf.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper with Memoization
ladders.desc: The recursive function that computes minimum jumps from a given index using memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP approach. It takes an index i and returns the minimum jumps from i to the last index, using memoization to store results. The intuition is that we can break the problem into subproblems: from index i, we need to find the minimum jumps among all possible next positions. By storing results in memo[i], we ensure each subproblem is solved only once. The algorithm checks if i is at or beyond the last index (base case returns 0). If memo[i] is already computed, return it. Otherwise, initialize min_jumps to infinity. For each possible jump length j from 1 to nums[i], recursively compute jumps from i+j, add 1 for the current jump, and update min_jumps. Store result in memo[i] and return it. Time complexity for this function called from a single index is O(n) in worst case (checking all jumps). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in the full solution to compute jumps from each index. Connection to L2: It implements the core DP recurrence relation. Common mistakes include incorrect base case (should be i >= n-1, not i == n-1), not using infinity for unreachable cases, or forgetting to add 1. Real code pattern: def helper(i, nums, memo): n = len(nums); if i >= n-1: return 0; if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; min_jumps = float('inf'); for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_idx = i + j; if next_idx < n: jumps = 1 + helper(next_idx, nums, memo); min_jumps = min(min_jumps, jumps); memo[i] = min_jumps; return memo[i];

examples.trace: Call helper(1). i=1, n=5. i=1 < 4, so not base case. memo[1] = -1, so compute. nums[1]=3. min_jumps = inf. Loop j=1: next_idx=2. helper(2) called. i=2, nums[2]=1. memo[2]=-1. min_jumps2=inf. Loop j=1: next_idx=3. helper(3) called. i=3, nums[3]=1. memo[3]=-1. min_jumps3=inf. Loop j=1: next_idx=4. helper(4) called. i=4 >=4, return 0. jumps=1+0=1. min_jumps3=1. memo[3]=1, return 1. Back to helper(2): jumps=1+1=2. min_jumps2=2. memo[2]=2, return 2. Back to helper(1): jumps=1+2=3. min_jumps=3. j=2: next_idx=3. helper(3) returns memo[3]=1. jumps=1+1=2. min_jumps=min(3,2)=2. j=3: next_idx=4. helper(4) returns 0. jumps=1+0=1. min_jumps=min(2,1)=1. memo[1]=1, return 1.

Call helper(0). i=0, n=5. i=0 <4. memo[0]=-1. nums[0]=3. min_jumps=inf. j=1: next_idx=1. helper(1) called. i=1, nums[1]=2. memo[1]=-1. min_jumps1=inf. j=1: next_idx=2. helper(2) called. i=2, nums[2]=1. memo[2]=-1. min_jumps2=inf. j=1: next_idx=3. helper(3) called. i=3, nums[3]=0. memo[3]=-1. min_jumps3=inf. j from 1 to 0: no iterations. min_jumps3 remains inf. memo[3]=inf, return inf. Back to helper(2): jumps=1+inf=inf. min_jumps2=inf. memo[2]=inf, return inf. Back to helper(1): jumps=1+inf=inf. min_jumps1=inf. j=2: next_idx=3. helper(3) returns inf. jumps=1+inf=inf. min_jumps1 remains inf. memo[1]=inf, return inf. Back to helper(0): jumps=1+inf=inf. min_jumps=inf. j=2: next_idx=2. helper(2) returns inf. jumps=1+inf=inf. j=3: next_idx=3. helper(3) returns inf. jumps=1+inf=inf. min_jumps remains inf. memo[0]=inf, return inf.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Minimum Jumps
ladders.desc: The mathematical relation that defines minimum jumps from an index in terms of reachable indices.
ladders.explanation: This core logic establishes the recurrence relation: minJumps(i) = 1 + min(minJumps(i+j)) for j from 1 to nums[i], where i+j < n. If i >= n-1, minJumps(i) = 0. If no valid j exists (e.g., nums[i]=0 and i < n-1), minJumps(i) = infinity. The intuition is that to reach the end from i, we must make one jump to some next position i+j, then optimally jump from there. By taking the minimum over all possible j, we find the optimal path. This relation exhibits optimal substructure—the optimal solution to the main problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. It leads to overlapping subproblems, making memoization beneficial. Time analysis: Evaluating this relation directly without memoization would be exponential. Space is O(n) for recursion. Connection to L1: The helper function implements this recurrence. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding array traversal and minimum operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting the +1 for the current jump, not handling the case where nums[i]=0 correctly, or incorrect bounds for j. Real code pattern: if i >= len(nums)-1: return 0; min_val = inf; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if i+j < len(nums): min_val = min(min_val, 1 + minJumps(i+j)); return min_val;

examples.trace: i=1, n=5. i <4, so not base case. nums[1]=3. Initialize min_val=inf. j=1: i+j=2. Need minJumps(2). From recurrence: i=2, nums[2]=1. j=1: i+j=3. Need minJumps(3). i=3, nums[3]=1. j=1: i+j=4. minJumps(4): base case returns 0. So minJumps(3)=1+0=1. Back: minJumps(2)=1+1=2. So for i=1, j=1: candidate=1+2=3. min_val=3. j=2: i+j=3. minJumps(3)=1. candidate=1+1=2. min_val=min(3,2)=2. j=3: i+j=4. minJumps(4)=0. candidate=1+0=1. min_val=min(2,1)=1. Return 1.

i=0, n=3. i<2. nums[0]=1. min_val=inf. j=1: i+j=1. Need minJumps(1). i=1, nums[1]=0. i<2, not base case. nums[1]=0, so loop j from 1 to 0: no iterations. min_val remains inf. Return inf for minJumps(1). Back to i=0: candidate=1+inf=inf. min_val=inf. Return inf.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Bounded Jumps
ladders.desc: Iterating through possible jump lengths from an array element with bounds checking.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves accessing an array element nums[i] and using its value to determine how many steps forward we can explore. The key is to traverse from index i to i+1, i+2, ..., i+nums[i], but ensuring we don't exceed array bounds. The intuition is that each position in the array gives a constraint on how far we can move, which is fundamental to jump game problems. The algorithm: given index i and array nums, loop j from 1 to nums[i], and for each, compute next index = i + j. If next index is within array length, process it; otherwise, break or ignore. Time complexity for this operation is O(k) where k = nums[i]. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This traversal is used in the recurrence to explore all possible next positions. Connection to L4: It relies on basic array indexing and loop operations. Common mistakes: Starting j from 0 instead of 1 (jump of 0 doesn't move), not checking if next index < len(nums), or using <= instead of < for array bounds. Real code pattern: for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_idx = i + j; if next_idx < len(nums): # process next_idx

examples.trace: i=1, nums[1]=3. Loop j from 1 to 3. j=1: next_idx = 1+1=2. Check 2 < 5: yes, process index 2. j=2: next_idx=1+2=3. 3<5: yes, process index 3. j=3: next_idx=1+3=4. 4<5: yes, process index 4. End loop.
i=1, nums[1]=0. Loop j from 1 to 0: no iterations. So no next indices to process.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Minimum in a List
ladders.desc: Computing the smallest value among a set of numbers, handling infinity for no values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves taking a set of numbers and determining the minimum value. In the context of jump game, we use it to find the smallest number of jumps among possible paths. The intuition is that we need to compare candidate jump counts and keep the smallest. The algorithm: initialize min_val to a very large number (e.g., infinity). For each candidate value, update min_val = min(min_val, candidate). If no candidates exist, min_val remains infinity. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of candidates. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied to the jump counts computed from traversed indices. Connection to L5: It relies on comparison operations. Common mistakes: Not initializing min_val to a large enough value, forgetting to handle the case with no candidates, or using wrong comparison logic. Real code pattern: min_val = float('inf'); for candidate in candidates: min_val = min(min_val, candidate);

examples.trace: Initialize min_val = inf. First candidate 3: min_val = min(inf, 3) = 3. Second candidate 2: min_val = min(3, 2) = 2. Third candidate 1: min_val = min(2, 1) = 1. Final min_val = 1.
Initialize min_val = inf. No candidates to process, so min_val remains inf.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using indices, with bounds checking.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to work with arrays. An array is a collection of elements accessible by indices starting from 0. To get the value at index i, use nums[i]. The intuition is that arrays allow random access to stored data. The algorithm: given array nums and index i, if i is within 0 and len(nums)-1, return nums[i]; otherwise, it's an error (out of bounds). Time and space are O(1). Connection to L4: This operation is used to get jump lengths from the array. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (common in some languages), not checking bounds leading to errors, or misunderstanding array length. Real code pattern: if 0 <= i < len(nums): value = nums[i]; else: # handle error

examples.trace: Array nums has length 5. Indices valid from 0 to 4. i=2 is within range. Access nums[2]: count from start: index 0 is 2, index 1 is 3, index 2 is 1. So return 1.
Array length is 3, valid indices 0,1,2. i=3 is out of bounds. Cannot access nums[3].

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Programming with Tabulation O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Iteratively fill a DP array from the end to the start to compute the minimum jumps for each index. Suitable when an iterative bottom-up solution is needed, though less efficient than greedy.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Jumps to End with DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) dynamic programming solution to find minimum jumps from start to end.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using dynamic programming with tabulation. We work backwards from the end to the beginning, filling a DP array where dp[i] represents the minimum number of jumps needed to reach the last index from index i. For each position i, we look at all reachable positions j (from i+1 to i+nums[i]), and dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[j]) among those j. The intuition is that from any position i, the optimal path is to make one jump to some reachable position j that has the minimum jumps to the end. This bottom-up approach ensures all subproblems are solved before the main problem. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case when nums[i] is large, and space is O(n) for the DP array. Common mistakes include not handling unreachable positions (should be set to infinity), off-by-one errors in the inner loop bounds, and not initializing dp[n-1] = 0. This connects to L1 as it uses the core DP recurrence relation throughout the entire array.

examples.trace: nums = [3,2,1,0,4], n=5. Initialize dp = [∞,∞,∞,∞,0]. i=3: nums[3]=0, j from 4 to 3 (no valid j since start>end), min remains ∞, dp[3]=∞. i=2: nums[2]=1, j from 3 to 3: dp[3]=∞, min=∞, dp[2]=∞. i=1: nums[1]=2, j from 2 to 3: dp[2]=∞, dp[3]=∞, min=∞, dp[1]=∞. i=0: nums[0]=3, j from 1 to 3: dp[1]=∞, dp[2]=∞, dp[3]=∞, min=∞, dp[0]=∞. Since dp[0]=∞, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence for a Single Index
ladders.desc: Compute minimum jumps from a specific index using reachable positions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recurrence relation: for a given index i, dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[j]) for all j where i < j ≤ i+nums[i] and j < n. The intuition is that from position i, we can jump 1 step to any position within nums[i] steps forward, and then need the minimum jumps from that new position to reach the end. We add 1 for the initial jump. This requires checking all possible next positions and finding the one with the smallest dp value. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(k) where k = min(nums[i], n-i-1), and space is O(1) excluding the dp array. Common mistakes include not checking that j stays within array bounds, and not handling cases where no valid j exists (should return infinity). This feeds into L0 as it's applied to every index in reverse order to build the complete solution.

examples.trace: Given i=1, nums[1]=3. Valid j range: from 2 to min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=2. j=3: dp[3]=1. j=4: dp[4]=0. Minimum dp[j] = 0 at j=4. dp[1] = 1 + 0 = 1.
Given i=2, nums[2]=2. Valid j range: from 3 to min(2+2,2)=2 (empty since start>end). No valid j, so min remains ∞. dp[2] = 1 + ∞ = ∞.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bottom-up DP with Tabulation
ladders.desc: Fill DP array from end to start using previously computed values.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the tabulation technique: we create a DP array of size n, initialize dp[n-1] = 0 (no jumps needed from end to end), then iterate i from n-2 down to 0, applying the recurrence dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[j]) for j in range(i+1, min(i+nums[i], n-1)+1). The intuition is that by processing indices in reverse order, we ensure that when computing dp[i], all dp[j] for j > i are already computed (since we go backwards). This is the essence of bottom-up dynamic programming—solving smaller subproblems first. Time complexity is O(n²) worst-case, space O(n). Common mistakes include incorrect loop direction (should be backwards), not initializing the last element properly, and not using min() to handle array bounds. This connects to L1 as it orchestrates multiple applications of the recurrence across the array.

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp=[∞,∞,0]. i=1: nums[1]=0, j from 2 to 1 (no valid j), min=∞, dp[1]=∞. i=0: nums[0]=2, j from 1 to 2: dp[1]=∞, dp[2]=0, min=0, dp[0]=1+0=1. Final dp=[1,∞,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Iterate through array indices with careful boundary conditions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array while respecting its bounds, particularly when computing ranges like from i+1 to i+nums[i]. We need to ensure the end index does not exceed n-1. The intuition is that in many array problems, we must access elements within valid indices to avoid errors. Here, we use min(i+nums[i], n-1) as the upper bound. Step-by-step: for each i, set start = i+1, end = min(i+nums[i], n-1), then iterate j from start to end inclusive. Time complexity depends on the traversal length, space O(1). Common mistakes include forgetting to check bounds, off-by-one errors (e.g., using < instead of ≤), and not handling cases where start > end (no iteration). This feeds into L2 as it's a critical part of implementing the DP recurrence safely.

examples.trace: i=0, nums[0]=2. start = i+1 = 1. end = min(i+nums[i], n-1) = min(0+2,4) = 2. Since start=1 ≤ end=2, valid j values are 1 and 2.
i=3, nums[3]=0. start = i+1 = 4. end = min(i+nums[i], n-1) = min(3+0,4) = 3. Since start=4 > end=3, no valid j values—empty range.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Minimum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum value in a contiguous subarray of an array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves scanning a portion of an array to find the smallest value. The intuition is simple: iterate through the specified indices, keep track of the current minimum, and update it when a smaller value is found. Step-by-step: initialize min_val to a large number (e.g., infinity), for each index in the range, if array[index] < min_val, set min_val = array[index]. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the subarray, space O(1). Common mistakes include not initializing min_val properly, not handling empty ranges (should return infinity or a sentinel), and incorrect comparison operators. This connects to L3 as it's used within the bounds-checked traversal to find min(dp[j]) for the DP recurrence.

examples.trace: Initialize min_val = ∞. Check j=1: arr[1]=1 < ∞, min_val=1. j=2: arr[2]=2 ≥ 1, min_val stays 1. j=3: arr[3]=1 ≥ 1, min_val stays 1. Final min_val=1.
Initialize min_val = ∞. Check j=1: arr[1]=∞ ≥ ∞, min_val stays ∞. Final min_val=∞.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Access array elements and iterate using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to work with arrays using indices. An array is a collection of elements accessible by position (index), starting from 0. The intuition is that we can store and retrieve values at specific positions. Step-by-step: to access an element, use array[index]; to iterate, use a loop like for i in range(n): access array[i]. Time complexity for traversal is O(n), space O(1) for the index variable. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., index ≥ n), confusing 0-based indexing, and incorrect loop ranges. This feeds into L4 as it enables scanning subarrays by controlling start and end indices.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Access arr[1] returns 20.
n=3. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0 print arr[0]=5, i=1 print arr[1]=3, i=2 print arr[2]=7.

approaches.name: Approach 4: Greedy BFS O(n)
approaches.summary: Simulate jumps as a BFS level-by-level, greedily extending the farthest reachable index at each step to minimize jumps. This is the optimal approach, leveraging local optimality for global minimum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game II with Greedy BFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find minimum jumps to reach last index using greedy BFS approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index of an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The greedy BFS approach models the jumps as a breadth-first search level-by-level, where each level corresponds to one jump. At each level, we track the farthest index reachable from any position within the current level's range. The algorithm maintains three key variables: jumps (count of jumps taken), current_end (the farthest index reachable with current jumps), and farthest (the farthest index reachable from positions in the current level). We iterate through the array, updating farthest as we go. When we reach current_end, we increment jumps and set current_end to farthest, simulating moving to the next BFS level. This works because at each jump, we greedily extend to the farthest possible position, which ensures minimal jumps overall—this is optimal due to the problem's greedy choice property. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once, and space is O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of updating farthest and managing jump boundaries. Common mistakes include not handling single-element arrays (where jumps should be 0) or incorrectly updating current_end before incrementing jumps. Real code pattern: Initialize jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0; loop i from 0 to n-2: update farthest = max(farthest, i+nums[i]); if i == current_end: jumps++, current_end = farthest; return jumps.

examples.trace: Start: jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0. i=0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2. i=current_end=0: jumps=1, current_end=2. i=1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4. i=2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4. i=current_end=2: jumps=2, current_end=4. Loop ends (n=5, i goes to 3). Return jumps=2.

Start: jumps=0, current_end=0, farthest=0. i=0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2. i=current_end=0: jumps=1, current_end=2. i=1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4. i=2: farthest=max(4,2+0)=4. i=3: farthest=max(4,3+1)=4. i=current_end=2: jumps=2, current_end=4. Loop ends. Return jumps=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Farthest Reach Update and Jump Boundary Management
ladders.desc: Core subroutine that updates the farthest reachable index and manages jump level transitions.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the greedy BFS approach. It involves two main operations: updating the farthest index reachable from the current position and checking when to increment the jump count. The intuition is that during a BFS level (one jump), we explore all positions within the current level's range and track the maximum reachable index from any of them. When we finish processing all positions in the current level (i.e., reach current_end), we move to the next level by incrementing jumps and setting the new level's boundary to the farthest index found. This ensures each jump extends as far as possible, minimizing total jumps. The algorithm uses a loop through indices, where at each index i: farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]) to update the farthest reach. Then, if i equals current_end (meaning we've processed all positions in the current jump level), we increment jumps and set current_end = farthest to start the next level. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, and space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is executed in the main loop to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include updating jumps before checking if i equals current_end or not handling the last index correctly. Real code pattern: for i in range(n-1): farthest = max(farthest, i+nums[i]); if i == current_end: jumps += 1; current_end = farthest.

examples.trace: Start: i=0: farthest=max(0,0+2)=2; i==current_end(0): jumps=1, current_end=2. i=1: farthest=max(2,1+3)=4; i!=current_end(2). i=2: farthest=max(4,2+1)=4; i==current_end(2): jumps=2, current_end=4. End.

Start: i=0: farthest=max(0,0+1)=1; i==current_end(0): jumps=1, current_end=1. i=1: farthest=max(1,1+2)=3; i==current_end(1): jumps=2, current_end=3. i=2: farthest=max(3,2+1)=3; i!=current_end(3). End.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Extension of Reachable Range
ladders.desc: The central insight of greedily extending the farthest reachable index at each step to minimize jumps.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical insight: to minimize jumps, at each jump, we should extend as far as possible from the current set of reachable positions. This is a greedy choice because selecting the farthest reachable index at each step leads to an overall optimal solution (minimum jumps). The reasoning is that if we can reach index X with k jumps, any index before X can also be reached with at most k jumps, so extending to the farthest possible index doesn't waste jumps. The algorithm simulates this by maintaining a 'current range' of indices reachable with the current number of jumps. From this range, we compute the farthest index reachable in one more jump, and then update the range to start from the next index after the current range up to that farthest index. This process repeats until we reach or exceed the last index. Time and space are O(1) per step, with overall O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by updating farthest and managing current_end. Common mistakes include not considering all indices in the current range when computing farthest. Real code pattern: While current_end < n-1: new_farthest = max(i+nums[i] for i in range(current_start, current_end+1)); jumps++; current_start = current_end+1; current_end = new_farthest.

examples.trace: Current range indices: [0]. From index 0: reachable = 0+2=2. Max reachable = 2. So new range is from 1 to 2. Jumps incremented to 1.
Current range indices: [1,2]. From index 1: reachable=1+3=4; from index 2: reachable=2+1=3. Max reachable = 4. New range is from 3 to 4. Jumps incremented to 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Level Simulation in Arrays
ladders.desc: Modeling jumps as levels in a breadth-first search traversal over array indices.
ladders.explanation: This concept involves simulating a BFS (breadth-first search) on the array, where each level corresponds to one jump. In BFS, we explore all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next depth. Here, 'nodes' are array indices, and an edge exists from index i to j if j is within i + nums[i]. The first level (depth 0) is just the start index 0. From level k, we can reach all indices that are within the jump range from any index in level k. The next level (k+1) consists of all indices reachable from level k that haven't been visited yet. The goal is to find the minimum depth (jumps) to reach the last index. This simulation avoids explicit queue usage by tracking the range of indices in the current level (current_start to current_end) and computing the range for the next level. The intuition is that BFS finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs, and here the graph is implicitly defined by jump distances. Time complexity is O(n) since each index is processed once, and space is O(1). Connection to L2: This BFS model enables the greedy extension by processing levels. Common mistakes include not updating the level range correctly or including already visited indices. Real code pattern: level=0; current_level_indices = [0]; while last index not in current_level_indices: next_level_indices = []; for i in current_level_indices: add all j from i+1 to i+nums[i] to next_level_indices; level++; current_level_indices = next_level_indices.

examples.trace: Level 0: indices [0]. From index 0: can jump to indices 1 and 2. So level 1: [1,2]. From index 1: can jump to indices 2,3,4 (but 2 already in level 1, so add 3,4); from index 2: can jump to index 3. So level 2: [3,4]. Since last index is 2, we stop at level 2.
Level 0: [0]. From 0: jump to 1. Level 1: [1]. From 1: jump to 2. Level 2: [2]. From 2: jump to 3. Level 3: [3]. Last index is 3, so 3 levels (jumps=3).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Reachable Indices from a Position
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: given an index and jump length, determine all indices reachable in one jump.
ladders.explanation: This operation is the building block for the jump game: from a current index i with jump length nums[i], we can reach any index j such that i < j ≤ i + nums[i]. In array terms, this means the reachable indices are in the range [i+1, i+nums[i]] inclusive, but bounded by the array length n-1. For example, if nums[i] = 3 and i = 0, we can jump to indices 1, 2, and 3. If i + nums[i] exceeds the last index, we can reach the last index directly. The intuition is that each position gives a 'jump radius'—the maximum distance you can move forward. This operation is used repeatedly in the algorithm to explore possible moves. Time complexity is O(1) to compute the range, and space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation defines the edges in the BFS simulation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., including i itself or missing the upper bound) or not clamping to array bounds. Real code pattern: reachable_start = i+1; reachable_end = min(i+nums[i], n-1); reachable_indices = list(range(reachable_start, reachable_end+1)).

examples.trace: From index 0, nums[0]=2. Reachable indices: start=0+1=1, end=min(0+2, 3)=2. So indices 1 and 2 are reachable.
From index 1, nums[1]=3. Reachable indices: start=1+1=2, end=min(1+3, 3)=3. So indices 2 and 3 are reachable. Note: index 4 is out of bounds since n=4, last index is 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Simple Loops
ladders.desc: Prerequisite: understanding array indices, accessing elements, and basic iteration.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves basic array operations: an array is a collection of elements accessible by indices starting from 0. For example, in array nums = [2,3,1], nums[0] = 2, nums[1] = 3, nums[2] = 1. The length n is 3, so valid indices are 0, 1, 2. A simple loop iterates through these indices, typically using a variable i that goes from 0 to n-1. At each step, we can access nums[i] and perform operations. This is essential for the jump game because we need to traverse the array to check jump possibilities. The intuition is that arrays allow random access to elements based on position, enabling efficient traversal. Time complexity for a loop is O(n), and space is O(1) if only a few variables are used. Connection to L4: This looping structure is used to compute reachable indices from each position. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for i in range(n): current_value = nums[i]; # do something with current_value.

examples.output: Iteration: i=0, value=2; i=1, value=3; i=2, value=1
examples.trace: Start with i=0: access nums[0] gives 2. Increment i to 1: access nums[1] gives 3. Increment i to 2: access nums[2] gives 1. Loop ends as i reaches n=3.

examples.output: Iteration: i=0, value=1; i=1, value=4; i=2, value=2
